text: Their figure 5 shows that a wind speed of 120 to 140mph at 18 hours prior to landfall typically results in surge of 15 to 18 feet. text: Why does this matter? Let's look at storm surge. According to Needham and Keim (2014), storm surge is affected by the hurricane's wind speeds 18 hours prior to landfall and closer. text: Normally this wouldn’t be an issue, as tropical cyclones tend to track from east to west, so a storm that forms west of 90W is unlikely to cross east of 90W. But Milton is a highly unusual exception, and ECMWF’s ensemble tracker apparently doesn’t handle crossing basins from west to east.

author: thomaspowell.com

text: There is a long technical explanation for why ECMWF’s tracker is having issues, but the short version is it partitions tropical cyclones into basins, and their Atlantic & Pacific basin border is delineated around 90W. The problem? Milton is west of 90W and crossing that boundary from west to east.
